name: Peracense Castle Loop from Villafranca del Campo
name_original: Castillo de Peracense – circular desde Villafranca del Campo
description: This difficult 31.0-mile touring cycling route from Villafranca del Campo takes you to the historic Castillo de Peracense.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Villafranca Del Campo traverse a diverse landscape, characterized by extensive agricultural plains, riverine environments along the Jiloca River, and elevated areas offering scenic views. The region features remnants of ancient holm oak forests, such as the Carrascal de la Matilla, providing shaded sections and varied terrain. Cyclists can explore routes alongside the Jiloca River, observing agrarian scenery, or venture into the foothills of Sierra Menera. This blend of flat valley paths and more challenging climbs to historical sites caters to a range of touring cyclists. Best touring cycling routes around Villafranca Del Campo The most popular touring cycling route is Peracense Castle Loop from Villafranca del Campo, a 31.0 miles (49.9 km) trail that takes 3 hours 24 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers significant elevation gain and leads to the historic Peracense Castle. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Peracense Castle loop from Peracense, a moderate 17.9 miles (28.9 km) path. This route provides views of the striking Peracense Castle and the surrounding Jiloca valley. Local touring cyclists also love the Peracense loop from Sierra Menera, a 16.1 miles (26.0 km) trail leading through the foothills of Sierra Menera, often completed in about 1 hour 35 minutes. Touring cycling around Villafranca Del Campo is defined by agricultural plains, the Jiloca River corridor, and ancient holm oak forests. The network offers options for different ability levels, from gentle river paths to more challenging climbs towards historical landmarks. The routes in Villafranca Del Campo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from a small number of reviews. More than 30 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Villafranca Del Campo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Villafranca Del Campo?
A: There are over 8 touring cycling routes available around Villafranca Del Campo, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Villafranca Del Campo?
A: The terrain around Villafranca Del Campo is diverse. You'll find extensive agricultural plains and relatively flat routes along the &lt;strong&gt;Jiloca River&lt;/strong&gt;, ideal for observing agrarian scenery and birdlife. There are also elevated areas and foothills of &lt;strong&gt;Sierra Menera&lt;/strong&gt;, which offer more challenging climbs and scenic views. Remnants of ancient holm oak forests, like the &lt;strong&gt;Carrascal de la Matilla&lt;/strong&gt;, provide shaded sections and varied landscapes.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easier ride?
A: Yes, while the guide lists 5 moderate and 3 difficult routes, the region's riverine environments along the Jiloca River offer relatively flat sections that can be enjoyed by those seeking an easier ride. These paths provide a gentle introduction to the area's natural beauty.

Q: What historical landmarks can I visit while touring cycling in Villafranca Del Campo?
A: The region is rich in history. You can cycle to the striking &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3925682" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peracense Castle&lt;/a&gt;, a 13th-century fortress offering spectacular views. Other notable historical sites include the restored &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4852508" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojos Negros Mill&lt;/a&gt;, a Roman dam and archaeological complex, and the 18th-century &lt;strong&gt;Church of San Juan Bautista&lt;/strong&gt;. Villafranca del Campo itself is situated on the historical &lt;strong&gt;Route of El Cid&lt;/strong&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Villafranca Del Campo?
A: Yes, several routes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e955659607/peracense-castle-loop-from-villafranca-del-campo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peracense Castle Loop from Villafranca del Campo&lt;/a&gt;, the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40008601" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peracense Castle loop from Peracense&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38157116" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peracense loop from Sierra Menera&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What are some of the more challenging touring cycling routes in the area?
A: For experienced touring cyclists, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e955659607/peracense-castle-loop-from-villafranca-del-campo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peracense Castle Loop from Villafranca del Campo&lt;/a&gt; stand out. This route covers nearly 50 km with significant elevation gain, leading to the historic Peracense Castle. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38148203" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monreal del Campo – Monreal del Campo loop from Monreal del Campo&lt;/a&gt;, which also features substantial climbs.

Q: What natural features or viewpoints can I enjoy on a touring cycling trip?
A: Beyond the agricultural plains and the &lt;strong&gt;Jiloca River&lt;/strong&gt;, you can explore remnants of ancient holm oak forests like the &lt;strong&gt;Carrascal de la Matilla&lt;/strong&gt;. The region is also close to the &lt;strong&gt;Gallocanta lagoon&lt;/strong&gt;, an important wetland for bird migration, especially for common cranes. Elevated sections of routes, particularly those leading to &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3925682" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peracense Castle&lt;/a&gt;, offer spectacular views of the Jiloca valley and surrounding landscapes.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Villafranca Del Campo?
A: The region's climate suggests that spring and autumn would be ideal for touring cycling, offering milder temperatures compared to the heat of summer. These seasons also provide pleasant conditions for observing the natural landscapes and birdlife, particularly around the Jiloca River and Gallocanta lagoon.

Q: Are there any specific routes that pass through or near interesting settlements?
A: Yes, several routes connect to or pass near interesting settlements. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38148203" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monreal del Campo – Monreal del Campo loop from Monreal del Campo&lt;/a&gt; explores the area around &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5058102" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monreal del Campo&lt;/a&gt;, which is known for its saffron museum. The broader region also includes towns like Alfaro, Peralta, and Marcilla, offering varied landscapes and river views.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy the most about touring cycling in Villafranca Del Campo?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Reviewers often praise the diverse terrain, from gentle river paths to challenging climbs, and the opportunity to combine physical activity with exploration of historical sites like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3925682" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peracense Castle&lt;/a&gt; and natural features.

Q: Can I find routes that offer a mix of historical and natural sights?
A: Absolutely. Many routes in Villafranca Del Campo are designed to showcase both the historical and natural richness of the area. For example, routes leading to &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3925682" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peracense Castle&lt;/a&gt; combine a visit to a significant historical landmark with scenic rides through the Jiloca valley and its natural surroundings. You can also find routes that pass by the Roman dam and archaeological sites, offering a blend of ancient history and outdoor exploration.
tips:
Peracense Castle has an exceptional history spanning several eras and societies. This impressive castle, built in the 13th century, took advantage of the natural conditions of the terrain and played a key role in the defence of the Kingdom of Aragon during the wars with Castile. A fascinating visit to the past! 🏰
I hope you enjoy your visit to Peracense Castle! 😊🏰
The road has mostly good pavement, asphalt and compacted gravel. The old train tracks can be seen in some of the sections.

faqs.question: What kind of challenges should I expect on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as difficult for touring cyclists. You should anticipate significant climbs and varied terrain, which may include both paved and unpaved sections. Some segments could be quite challenging, potentially requiring very good fitness or even needing to push your bike.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is approximately 49.88 km (31 miles) long and typically takes around 3 hours and 24 minutes to complete on a touring bicycle, depending on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied, featuring rolling hills and a mix of surfaces. While some parts may be well-paved, expect unpaved and difficult segments. The region is characterized by gentle hills, limestone outcrops, and areas covered with holm oaks.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: The primary highlight is the impressive Peracense Castle, a 13th-century fortress built from distinctive red 'rodeno' sandstone. You'll also encounter the Ojos Negros Greenway, and may pass by historical sites like the Celtiberian archaeological site of Las Saletas and a medieval dam near Villafranca del Campo.

faqs.question: Where can I park and start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is a circular one starting from Villafranca del Campo. You can typically find parking within the town to begin your ride. Specific trailhead parking details are not provided, but starting from the town center is a common approach for circular routes.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: While specific recommendations aren't given, generally, spring and autumn offer the most pleasant cycling conditions in this region, avoiding the extreme heat of summer and potential winter cold or snow. Always check local weather conditions before you go.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No specific permits are required to cycle this route. However, if you plan to enter Peracense Castle, there may be an entrance fee, so it's advisable to check their official website for current information.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route itself is outdoors, specific rules regarding dogs on cycling routes can vary. It's generally advisable to keep dogs on a leash, especially near roads or populated areas. If you plan to visit any specific attractions like the castle, check their individual pet policies.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay near the route?
faqs.answer: Villafranca del Campo, as the starting and ending point, is likely to offer some basic services, including cafes or restaurants. For accommodation, you would typically find options in Villafranca del Campo or nearby towns in the Teruel region.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You can expect diverse and historically rich scenery. This includes gentle hills dotted with holm oaks, striking limestone outcrops with dramatic folds, and expansive views over the Jiloca depression. The highlight is the unique red sandstone landscape surrounding Peracense Castle.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with sections of the Via verde Ojos Negros. You will also find small portions that overlap with the Sierra Menera and Camino Natural Santander Mediterráneo. Tramo CN VV Ojos Negros: Santa Eulalia - Puerto Escandón.
